ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general cereal beverage class; no specific multigrain cereal drink entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW covers cereal-based beverages broadly; no multigrain-specific sub-code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for multigrain cereal drink; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 mL of prepared multigrain cereal drink, typically made by dissolving 25–30 g of powder in hot water.

nutrient_source.title: Multigrain Cereal Drink — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Multigrain Cereal Drink (cr_id D04010006). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific multigrain cereal drink formulation. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous instant cereal/grain-based beverages with similar carbohydrate composition (11g/100mL, sugar 4.9g/100mL, fibre 1.15g/100mL) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Instant oat-based and mixed-grain beverages in the database cluster in the GI 55–70 range. GL per 250 mL serving = (65 × 27.55g available carbohydrate per 250 mL) / 100 ≈ 18.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar content (4.94 g/100 mL) in a sweetened instant beverage format; low sodium (9 mg/100 mL) is favourable but does not offset the added sugar concern per AHA dietary guidelines for hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should choose the unsweetened variant of multigrain cereal drink or prepare it without added sugar, as the sweetened formulation contributes added sugar that adversely affects blood pressure regulation per AHA guidelines, despite its low sodium content of 9 mg/100 mL.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) classification with 4.94 g added sugar per 100 mL and rapidly absorbed carbohydrates (11.02 g/100 mL) with insufficient dietary fibre (1.15 g/100 mL) to moderate postprandial glucose response; estimated GI of 65 (medium) yields a glycemic load of approximately 18 per 250 mL serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this sweetened multigrain cereal drink as it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage per ADA guidelines; if consumed, limit to 150–200 mL and pair with a protein source to blunt the glycemic response, or substitute with an unsweetened multigrain cereal powder preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (4.94 g/100 mL) in a beverage format raises cardiovascular risk through triglyceride elevation and inflammatory pathways; low sodium (9 mg/100 mL) and low saturated fat (~0.3 g/100 mL) are favourable, but the wholegrain benefit is partially offset by the added sugar and low fibre density.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, choose the unsweetened multigrain cereal drink variant to retain the wholegrain fibre benefit while eliminating the added sugar that contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar with fibre only 1.15 g/100 mL and no meaningful anti-inflammatory factors (no omega-3, no polyphenols, no meaningful antioxidants) provides no positive anti-inflammatory value; neutrality is insufficient for RA suitability per EULAR dietary principles.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose the unsweetened multigrain cereal drink variant and consider adding anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric or ginger, as the sweetened formulation provides no anti-inflammatory benefit and the added sugar may promote low-grade inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Multigrain cereal drink is a processed grain-based beverage popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically prepared by dissolving an instant powder blend of multiple grains — commonly wheat, oats, barley, rice, and corn — in hot water. The resulting drink has a mild, slightly sweet, nutty cereal flavour with a smooth, lightly viscous texture. It is commonly consumed as a breakfast beverage or morning meal replacement. Per 100 mL of prepared drink, it provides 62 kcal, 1.55 g protein, 1.37 g fat, 11.02 g carbohydrate (of which 4.94 g is sugar), 1.15 g dietary fibre, and 9 mg sodium. The product is low in sodium and relatively low in calories but contains added sugar, classifying it as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per ADA and AHA guidelines. The estimated glycemic index is 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 18 per 250 mL serving. Clinically, this drink is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its SSB classification and moderate glycemic load; the unsweetened variant is preferred. For hypertension, the low sodium is favourable but added sugar remains a concern. The wholegrain base provides modest fibre but insufficient to confer meaningful anti-inflammatory or glycaemic-moderating benefit in the sweetened form. It is suitable for vegetarians, vegans, and halal diets, but contains gluten from wheat, oats, and barley.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics drink multigrain cereal drink?
How many calories in multigrain cereal drink?
Is multigrain cereal drink good for weight loss?
What grains are in multigrain cereal drink?
Is multigrain cereal drink suitable for hypertension?
Is multigrain cereal drink high in sugar?
Can I drink multigrain cereal drink for breakfast?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D04010006) at confidence 0.95. All primary macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from the approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database cluster for similar instant grain beverages, reducing overall confidence for the GI component only; overall record confidence remains high given exact HPB provenance for all nutritional data.
notes: calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 per HPB source — these are confirmed zero values as reported in the HPB Foodo database for this product formulation. saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.3 g/100 g based on typical fat profile of mixed grain beverages (HPB source does not disaggregate fat fractions); monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient, fatty acid disaggregation, and moisture data for this multigrain cereal drink are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable instant mixed-grain cereal beverages; no direct GI measurement for this specific product was found in approved sources.
